lesson.subtitle: Aula 4 -- Managing Vendors -- Negotiation Basics
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Rafael Pelizaro | Aula 4 -- Managing Vendors

reading.rtitle: Renewing the Software Contract — A Vendor Negotiation
paras: Every year, Rafael has to renew the licenses for his team's project software. This time, the first quote from the supplier is fifteen percent over his budget, so he cannot simply accept it. Before the call, he prepares his proposal carefully. He decides which terms matter most to him and the one price he would be happy with. A good manager never opens a negotiation without a plan.

On the call, Rafael stays calm and polite. He thanks the supplier for the quote, but explains that the price is a little high for this year. "Would you consider a discount," he asks, "for a two-year commitment instead of one?" The supplier is flexible. She offers ten percent off for the longer deal, but she asks for faster payment in return. Rafael makes a counteroffer: he would accept the longer term and the faster payment, but only if support is included at no extra cost.

After a few minutes, they reach an agreement that works for both sides. Rafael gets a better price and full support; the supplier gets a two-year deal. Nobody has to lose. The secret is simple: know your terms, stay polite, and always be ready to offer something in return. A negotiation is not a fight — it is a conversation where both people look for a deal they would both sign.
reading.source: Adapted for class use from an internal IT vendor-management note

gist.prompt: What is the best one-line summary of this text?
choices: The supplier refused to move on the price, so Rafael walked away with no deal.
After a calm, well-prepared negotiation, Rafael and the supplier reach a two-year agreement that works for both sides.
Rafael accepted the first quote exactly as it was, without any changes.

items: Rafael accepts the supplier's first quote without changing anything.
The quote is over budget, so he prepares and negotiates instead of accepting it.
Rafael prepares his proposal and his terms before the call.
He decides which terms matter most and the one price he would be happy with.
The supplier offers a discount in exchange for a longer commitment.
She offers ten percent off for a two-year deal, and asks for faster payment in return.
In the end, only the supplier is happy with the deal.
They reach an agreement that works for both sides — nobody has to lose.
Before a negotiation, how do you decide which terms matter most to you?
Rafael offers something in return for the discount. Why is that often better than just asking for a lower price?
When did you last negotiate with a supplier or vendor at work? What did each side give and get?

listenings.text: Hi, this is Rafael with a quick update after my call with the software supplier, before our budget meeting. The good news is that we have a deal, and it is a better one than the first quote. You remember the problem: their opening price was about fifteen percent over our budget for this year, so I could not just accept it. I went into the call with a clear plan. I knew which terms mattered most to us, and I knew the one number I would be happy with. On the call I stayed polite. I thanked them for the proposal, but I was honest that the price was too high. Then I asked the key question: would they consider a discount for a longer commitment? They were flexible. They offered ten percent off if we signed for two years instead of one, but in return they wanted faster payment. So I made a counteroffer. I said we would accept the two-year term and the faster payment, but only if full support was included at no extra cost. After a few minutes, we reached an agreement that works for both sides. We get a better price and full support; they get a longer deal. I will send the new quote and the terms around before the meeting. Thanks.

Hello Rafael, this is Sofia from the vendor team, following up on our call with a revised offer in writing, as you asked. Thank you again for a friendly and professional conversation. Here is where we have landed. On the price, we are happy to give you the ten percent discount we discussed, in exchange for the two-year commitment. On payment, we would still like the faster thirty-day terms, and I can see from our notes that you are comfortable with that. And on your main request, yes, we agree: full technical support is now included at no extra cost for the whole length of the contract. So, to recap the agreement: a two-year deal, ten percent off the original quote, thirty-day payment, and support included. I think that is a fair result for both sides. I will put all of this into a formal proposal and send it over for signature this week. If anything looks different from what you expected, just give me a call and we will sort it out. Looking forward to working with your team. Thanks, Rafael.
